questions.question: Differentiate between simplex, half‑duplex, and full‑duplex transmission modes. For each mode, give one practical example of a network device or scenario where it is used.
keyPoints: Simplex: data flows in one direction only (e.g., keyboard to computer, serial console).
Half‑duplex: data can travel both directions but not simultaneously (e.g., walkie‑talkie, half‑duplex Ethernet hub).
Full‑duplex: simultaneous bidirectional communication (e.g., telephone, full‑duplex Ethernet switch).
Transmission mode impacts design of physical‑layer devices and network performance (collision handling).

questions.question: Explain the roles of a repeater, a hub, and a modem in a network. How do they differ in terms of OSI layer, signal handling, and effect on collision domains?
keyPoints: Repeater operates at the Physical layer (Layer 1), amplifies/reshapes weak or corrupted signals, does not filter traffic.
Hub also operates at Layer 1; it copies incoming bits to all ports, creating a single shared collision domain.
Modem (Modulator‑Demodulator) operates at the Physical layer, converting digital data to analog for transmission over analog links (e.g., telephone, cable) and back to digital.
Repeater, hub, and modem all extend the network’s physical span but do not segment broadcast or collision domains.

questions.question: Describe how a Layer‑2 Ethernet switch and a Layer‑3 router function. Discuss their differences in terms of the OSI layers they operate at, the type of addressing they use, how they handle frames versus packets, and the impact on collision and broadcast domains. Also, explain why full‑duplex operation on a switch eliminates collisions and how this improves network efficiency.
keyPoints: Switch works at Data Link layer (Layer 2) using MAC addresses; it learns MAC tables, forwards frames only to the destination port, and creates separate collision domains per port.
Router works at Network layer (Layer 3) using IP addresses; it routes packets between different networks, segments broadcast domains, and makes forwarding decisions based on routing tables.
Full‑duplex on a switch allows simultaneous send and receive on each port, removing the CSMA/CD collision mechanism and doubling potential bandwidth.
Switching improves throughput and security by micro‑segmentation, while routing provides inter‑network connectivity and path selection.

questions.question: Compare and contrast the functions of a hub, a switch, and a router in a typical campus network. Include discussion of transmission modes each device supports, OSI layer operation, effect on bandwidth utilization, and illustrate how traffic flows when a host in one VLAN communicates with a host in a different VLAN.
keyPoints: Hub (Layer 1) repeats incoming bits to all ports, supports only half‑duplex (or full‑duplex but still shared bandwidth), creates a single collision domain.
Switch (Layer 2) forwards frames based on MAC addresses, supports full‑duplex per port, creates separate collision domains, and can segment broadcast domains when VLANs are configured.
Router (Layer 3) routes packets based on IP addresses, separates broadcast domains, can connect different LAN technologies, and performs NAT, firewalling, and path selection.

When a host in VLAN 1 (on a switch) sends data to a host in VLAN 2, the traffic must be routed by a Layer‑3 device (router or Layer‑3 switch); the router examines the IP header, makes routing decisions, and forwards the packet to the appropriate VLAN interface.
